description: Cross-paper edges for the Kakeya / restriction DAG. from/to are '<arxiv_id>:<node_id>'; direction is subject-verb-object (from {type} to). The node math/0509262 (Bennett–Carbery–Tao, multilinear restriction and Kakeya) is a SHARED node with the bourgain-demeter-decoupling DAG and the load-bearing hub of the harmonic-analysis region. The hub is a genuine dependency cluster, not a single thematic edge: BCT proves the near-optimal (ε-loss) multilinear Kakeya bound (thm:1.15); Guth improves it to the endpoint (0811.2251:thm:2 improves thm:1.15) and later re-derives the endpoint by a shorter argument (1409.4683:thm:1 re_derives 0811.2251:thm:1); and on the decoupling side the ℓ² decoupling theorem is proved from multilinear restriction (1403.5335:thm:6.1 imports_result math/0509262:thm:1.16). Guth's restriction estimate descends from the method lineage (2402.03470, a references edge — see note), and Wang–Zahl complete the full Kakeya conjecture by an independent method (thematic edge).

edges.note: Guth proves the ENDPOINT multilinear Kakeya inequality (thm:2, estimate form) via the polynomial ham-sandwich theorem, removing the $\epsilon$-loss in Bennett–Carbery–Tao's near-optimal bound (thm:1.15). This is the load-bearing input that makes the hub a genuine dependency cluster rather than a single thematic edge. Both are multilinear-Kakeya papers shared with the decoupling DAG.

Guth's 2014 'short proof' re-derives the endpoint multilinear Kakeya inequality he first proved in 0811.2251 (thm:2, estimate form), replacing the polynomial ham-sandwich argument with a streamlined induction on scales. Same conclusion, shorter proof. (Matches the typing already recorded in the decoupling DAG.)

Guth's short proof re-derives the Bennett–Carbery–Tao multilinear Kakeya inequality (thm:1.15) by a streamlined induction-on-scales argument. Both are multilinear-Kakeya papers shared with the decoupling DAG.

Guth's polynomial-partitioning restriction estimate descends from the multilinear-Kakeya method lineage; the study guide situates it relative to multilinear Kakeya (§1, cite [multkakeya]). NOTE: this exposition proves its transversal estimate via Wongkew's variety-neighborhood bound and polynomial partitioning, not via BCT directly — so this is a method-lineage reference, not a load-bearing dependency. (Guth's original restriction papers do use multilinear restriction for the broad estimate; those papers are not yet parsed in the registry.)

Wang–Zahl's resolution of the Kakeya set conjecture in $\mathbb{R}^3$ and Bennett–Carbery–Tao's near-optimal multilinear Kakeya (thm:1.15) are milestones on the same Kakeya problem. Wang–Zahl prove the full conjecture by an independent grain-structure / sum-product method, so there is no logical dependency on multilinear Kakeya — the relation is thematic.

Guth's survey states the general Kakeya conjecture (conjecture:1.1) that multilinear Kakeya (thm:1.15) partially addressed; the survey situates the Wang–Zahl proof against this prior progress. Thematic adjacency, not dependency.
